lesson.title: Rights During Arrest & Detention
lesson.explanation: Section 35 of the Constitution: you have the right to remain silent, the right to be informed of charges in a language you understand, the right to a lawyer (Legal Aid SA: 0800 110 110), the right to be brought before a court within 48 hours, and the right to bail unless the interests of justice require otherwise. Police must identify themselves and show a warrant (unless caught in the act). You may not be tortured or treated inhumanely. You can refuse to answer questions without a lawyer present. If rights are violated: report to IPID (Independent Police Investigative Directorate) 012 399 0000.

practice_problems.question: What is the first step when beginning work in Rights During Arrest & Detention?
Describe the key regulation or standard that governs Rights During Arrest & Detention in South Africa.
practice_problems.answer: See lesson content for specific SA regulations.
hints: Think about SANS standards or Acts
practice_problems.explanation: SA has specific standards for each trade.

misconceptions.misconception: You don't need formal training for Rights During Arrest & Detention
misconceptions.correction: Proper training and certification are required for safety and legal compliance in SA.
misconceptions.why_students_think_this: People see informal work and assume no qualifications are needed.
